slides.title: Discussion on Tutorial: Q3
slides.content:
(a) Suppose a crime has been committed. Blood is found at the scene for which there is
no innocent explanation. The blood of a type which is present in 1% of the population.
The prosecutor claims: “There is a 1% chance that the defendant would have the crime
blood type if he were innocent. Thus, there is a 99% chance that he is guilty”. This is
known as the prosecutor’s fallacy. Explain in detail what is wrong with this argument.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

(b) Further, the defender claims: “The crime occurred in a city of 800,000 people. The
blood type would be found in approximately 8000 people. The evidence has provided a
probability of just 1 in 8000 that the defendant is guilty and thus has no relevance.” This
is known as the defender’s fallacy. Explain in detail what is wrong with this argument?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Linear Classifiers:
slides.content:
✓ Perceptron Algorithm and its Variants.
✓ Least Square Methods for Optimal Linear Classifiers.
✓ The Bias-Variance Dilemma.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Linear Classifiers: Simple yet Important
slides.content:
Intuitively, what is the advantage(s) of linear classifiers?
Simplicity and computational attractiveness.
Of course, linear classifiers are NOT perfect.
First assume that all feature vectors from the available classes
can be classified correctly using a linear classifier.
The goal: design an “optimal linear classifier” by adopting an
appropriate optimality criterion.
Further assumptions (more general case) will be that feature vectors
may not be classified correctly – the risk of mis-classification.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Discriminant Functions and Decision Surfaces
slides.content:
∗
Bayes decision rule: 𝜋 𝑥 = arg min 𝜌 𝑎 𝑥 = arg max 𝑝(𝑦|𝑥)
𝑎∈𝒜 𝑦∈𝒴
Equivalent to partitioning the feature space into 𝐶 regions, for a
classification task with 𝐶 classes.
First focus the binary classification case: 𝒴 = {1,2}, thus the partition
will result in 2 regions.
We can safely assume that the two regions are contiguous – they are
thus separated by a “decision surface” (also known as a decision
hypersurface) in the feature space.
The decision surface is characterized by the following equation:
𝑝 𝑦 𝑥 − 𝑝 𝑦 𝑥 = 0
1 2
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

𝑝 𝑦 𝑥 − 𝑝 𝑦 𝑥 = 0
1 2
From one side of the surface the difference > 0 and vice versa;
Often, more convenient to work with an equivalent function:
𝑔 𝑥 ≡ 𝑓 𝑝 𝑦 𝑥 . 𝑓 ⋅ is a monotonically increasing function;
𝑖 𝑖
𝑔 (𝑥) is defined as a discriminant function.
𝑖
To classify input 𝑥 as the 𝑖-th class, we adopt the decision test:
𝑝 𝑦 |𝑥 > 𝑝 𝑦 𝑥 . This is a general function for ∀𝑗 ≠ 𝑖.
𝑖 𝑗
With discriminant function, the above decision test is restated: classify
as 𝑖-th class if 𝑔 𝑥 > 𝑔 𝑥 , ∀𝑗 ≠ 𝑖.
𝑖 𝑗
Decision hypersurfaces are described as 𝑔 𝑥 ≡ 𝑔 𝑥 − 𝑔 𝑥 = 0.
𝑖𝑗 𝑖 𝑗

For a binary classification task, we may consider leveraging a

The decision hypersurface in the feature space is thus a hyperplane:

slides.title: The Perceptron Algorithm: Optimization
slides.content:
∗𝑇
Compute the unknown parameters 𝑤 , 𝑖 = 0,1, … , 𝐷 for 𝒘 𝑥 = 0.
𝑖
View the problem as a typical optimization task:
An appropriate cost function;
An algorithmic optimization scheme to optimize the cost function.
Any simple but still relatively accurate cost function?
The perceptron cost: 𝐽 𝒘 = σ 𝛿 𝒘 𝑇 𝑥 , where 𝑀 is the subset of
𝑥∈𝑀 𝑥
mis-classified training vectors. Set 𝛿 = −1, ∀𝑥 ∈ 𝑦 and 𝛿 = 1, ∀𝑥 ∈ 𝑦 .
𝑥 1 𝑥 2
When the cost function takes its minimum value, which is 0, can safely
assume that a solution has been obtained, since all training feature
vectors are correctly classified.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

𝑇
The perceptron cost: 𝐽 𝒘 = σ 𝛿 𝒘 𝑥 . How to optimize?
𝑥∈𝑀 𝑥
The perceptron cost function is continuous and piecewise linear.
If 𝒘 changes smoothly, the cost 𝐽 𝒘 changes linearly until the point at
which there is a change in the number of misclassified vectors.
At these points the gradient is not defined, and the gradient function is
discontinuous.
Next: how to optimize the perceptron cost (to obtain the min value)?
Iterative minimization in the spirit of gradient descent. Defined at all
points where the gradient function is continuous:
𝜕𝐽 𝒘
𝒘 𝑡 + 1 = 𝒘 𝑡 − 𝜌 ቚ

In the gradient descent scheme,
the correction of the parameters
takes place in the direction that
decreases the value of the cost
function.
In reality, most cost functions are
much more complicated: there
exists local and global minimum,
as well as saddle points.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Variants of the Perceptron Algorithm
slides.content:
A simpler form of the perceptron algorithm – cyclical form:
The training data enter the algorithm cyclically, one by one.
If the algorithm has not converged after the presentation of all the
samples once, then the procedure keeps repeating until convergence,
i.e, when all training samples have been classified correctly.
Let 𝑤 𝑡 be the weight vector estimated and 𝑥 the corresponding data
𝑡
presented at the 𝑡-th iteration step, the algorithm is stated as:
𝑇
𝑤 𝑡 + 1 = 𝑤 𝑡 + 𝜌𝑥 , if 𝑥 ∈ 𝑦 and 𝑤 𝑡 𝑥 ≤ 0 (note similarity);
𝑡 𝑡 1 𝑡
𝑇
𝑤 𝑡 + 1 = 𝑤 𝑡 − 𝜌𝑥 , if 𝑥 ∈ 𝑦 and 𝑤 𝑡 𝑥 ≥ 0;
𝑡 𝑡 2 𝑡
𝑤 𝑡 + 1 = 𝑤 𝑡 , otherwise.
How to interpret this variant of the perceptron algorithm?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

𝑇
𝑤 𝑡 + 1 = 𝑤 𝑡 + 𝜌𝑥 , if 𝑥 ∈ 𝑦 and 𝑤 𝑡 𝑥 ≤ 0;
𝑡 𝑡 1 𝑡
𝑇
𝑤 𝑡 + 1 = 𝑤 𝑡 − 𝜌𝑥 , if 𝑥 ∈ 𝑦 and 𝑤 𝑡 𝑥 ≥ 0;
𝑡 𝑡 2 𝑡
𝑤 𝑡 + 1 = 𝑤 𝑡 , otherwise.
If the current training sample is classified correctly, no action is
taken. If the sample is misclassified, the weight vector is
corrected by adding/subtracting an amount proportional to 𝑥 .
𝑡
The algorithm belongs to a more general algorithmic family: the
reward and punishment schemes.
If the classification is correct, the reward is that no action is taken.
Otherwise, the punishment is the cost of correction.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.content:
4 points are as shown in the feature space: blue being
class 1 𝑦 and black being class 2 𝑦 .
1 2
How to design a linear classifier using the perceptron algorithm in
its reward and punishment form?
Set 𝜌 = 1 and initial weight vector 𝒘 0 = 0, 0, 0 𝑇
Since for four consecutive steps no correction is needed, all points are
correctly classified and the algorithm terminates.
𝑇
The solution is 𝒘 = −1, 1, 0 , which corresponds to the linear classifier
− 𝑥 + 𝑥 = 0

slides.title: The Perceptron
slides.content:
The goal of the perceptron algorithm: obtain a linear classifier
that is applicable to new (and unknown) data:
I.e., classify the new data into the two classes (binary classification).
𝑇
IF 𝑤 𝑥 + 𝑤 > 0, assign 𝑥 to 𝑦 ;
0 1
𝑇
ELSE IF 𝑤 𝑥 + 𝑤 < 0, assign 𝑥 to 𝑦 ;
0 2
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Perceptron: Details
slides.content:
Let’s describe the perceptron in detail:
The elements of the feature vector 𝑥 are applied to the input nodes (blue).
Then each one is multiplied by the corresponding weights known as
synaptic weights or simply synapses (orange).
The products are summed up with the threshold 𝑤 . The result then goes
0
through a nonlinear device, which implements the activation function 𝑓.
Wide range of choices for 𝑓, example: the hard limiter step function
𝑓 𝑧 = −1 if 𝑧 < 0 and 𝑓 𝑧 = 1 if 𝑧 > 0.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Perceptron
slides.content:
This basic network is known as a
perceptron or neuron.
Perceptrons are simple examples of the learning machines –
i.e., structures whose free parameters are updated by a
learning algorithm, such as the perceptron algorithm, to “learn”
a specific task, based on a set of training data.
The perceptron can be used as the basic building element for
more complex learning networks.
Sometimes a neuron with a hard limiter device is referred to as
a McCulloch-Pitts neuron.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Extending to Multi-Class
slides.content:
Previously, we default our task as a binary classification task –
how can we generalize to multi-class (𝑀-class) scenarios?
Generalize in the simplest manner:
A linear discriminant function (defined by a weight vector 𝒘 ) is defined
𝑖
for each of the 𝑀 classes.
Input feature (data) 𝑥 is classified as the 𝑖-th class if the following holds:
𝑇 𝑇
𝒘 𝑥 > 𝒘 𝑥, ∀𝑗 ≠ 𝑖
𝑖 𝑗
Discussion: how can we construct a linear classifier that could perform
this comparison across all classes in one-shot?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Linearly Separable?
slides.content:
From previous slides, we could observe how simple the
formulation of a linear classifier is.
Our discussion: all data can be classified as one of the classes;
I.e., a strong assumption that the classes are linearly separable.
In many cases, the class are not linearly
separable – but still want to leverage the
simplicity of linear classifiers.
Suboptimal, but simple → best of all suboptimals;
Compute the weight vectors under a suitable
optimality criterion;
A simple criterion to start with – mean square error.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Classification with (Mean Square) Error
slides.content:
Our goal: design a linear classifier so that the desired outputs
of the data are ±1 – but with errors.
The true output will not always be equal to the desired output.
𝑇
Given input 𝑥, the actual output is 𝒘 𝑥, the desired output is 𝑦(𝑥).
𝒘 is computed to minimize the mean square error (MSE) between the
actual output and the desired output.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Sum of Error Squares Estimation
slides.content:
A criterion closely related to the MSE is the sum of error
squares or simply the least squares error (LSE) criterion:
𝑁 𝑁 𝑁
2 2
𝑇 𝑇 2
𝐽 𝒘 = ෍ 𝑦 𝑥 − 𝑥 𝒘 = ෍ 𝑦 − 𝑥 𝒘 ≡ ෍ 𝑒
𝑖 𝑖 𝑖 𝑖 𝑖
𝑖 𝑖 𝑖
Compare with MSE?
Errors btw the desired output and the true output are summed over all
the training feature vectors, instead of averaging out.
Overcome the need for explicit knowledge of the underlying probability
distribution functions (pdfs).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Hence the equation for optimal weight vector is formulated as:
𝑇 𝑇 𝑇 −1 𝑇

𝑇

𝑇

vector that minimizes the sum of squared errors.
Note that the LSE solution tends to the MSE solution when 𝑁 is large!
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Over- and Under-Determined Systems
slides.content:
𝑇 𝑇 𝑇 −1 𝑇
𝑋 𝑋 𝑤ෝ = 𝑋 𝑌 ⇒ 𝑤ෝ = 𝑋 𝑋 𝑋 𝑌
During the computation of the above equation, we assume 𝑁 > 𝑙, i.e., we
have more samples than the number of features per sample.
We have more than enough equations to determine the linear classifier –
denoted as an over-determined system.
Question: what if 𝑁 < 𝐷, i.e., we do NOT have enough samples?
There will be more than one unique solution!
We then need to constrain the search. Assume (𝑋𝑋 𝑇 ) is invertible, denote 𝑤 = 𝑋 𝑇 𝑎,
then we have 𝑋𝑋 𝑇 𝑎 = 𝑦 from 𝑋𝑤 = 𝑦.

slides.title: The Capabilities of Linear Classifiers
slides.content:
𝑇
As discuss, the linear classifiers 𝑔 𝑥 = 𝒘 𝑥 + 𝑤 can be viewed as
0
learning machines which attempt to estimate the corresponding class
label 𝑦.
In practice, (and discussed for LSE estimation), the functions 𝑔 ⋅ are
estimated using a finite training dataset denoted as 𝒟 = { 𝑦 , 𝑥 , 𝑖 =
𝑖 𝑖
1,2, … , 𝑁} and a suited method (MSE/LSE).
To emphasize the dependency of 𝑔 over 𝒟, we write 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 .
The ultimate goal of 𝑔 is to be applicable to new data – the capabilities of
𝑔 to approximate the real class and how this is affected by the finite
training set?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Note that the approximation of the real class may be very good for a
specific training data set but very bad for another.
The effectiveness of 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 can be evaluated by computing its mean
square deviation from the desired optimal value (essentially, the MSE):
2
𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝑦 𝑥
𝒟
The above formulation can be further re-formulated as:
2
𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝑦 𝑥
𝒟
2
2
= 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝑦 𝑥 + 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟
𝒟 𝒟 𝒟
The first term is the contribution of the bias and the second is the
contribution of the variance.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: End of Week 4
slides.content:
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Bias-Variance Dilemma: Bias
slides.content:
2
Bias is formulated as: 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝑦 𝑥 .
𝒟
Bias refers to the degree to which a model is able to accurately
capture the underlying patterns in a dataset.
Layman term: the diversion from original answer is known as Bias.
We say 𝑔(⋅) is unbiased when 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 = 𝑦 𝑥 .
𝒟
A model with high bias is said to be underfitting the data, as it is not
able to capture the complexity of the true underlying relationship.
How can we intuitively know if 𝑔 is of high or low bias?
High bias: poor performance on both training data and unseen data.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Bias-Variance Dilemma: Variance
slides.content:
2
Variance is formulated as: 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 .
𝒟 𝒟
Variance refers to the degree to which a model is able to adapt
to new data.
Layman term: the Variance measures the “spread” of a distribution.
A model with high variance is said to be overfitting the data, as it is
too complex and has learned to fit the noise in the training data as
well as the underlying patterns.
How can we intuitively know if 𝑔 is of high or low variance?
High variance: much poorer performance on unseen data compared to
the training data. (Usually training performance is quite descent)
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Bias-Variance Dilemma
slides.content:
Suppose we are trying to predict red target below.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

2
𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝑦 𝑥
𝒟
2
2
= 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝑦 𝑥 + 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟 − 𝔼 𝑔 𝑥; 𝒟
𝒟 𝒟 𝒟
For a finite data set, there is a trade-off between these two terms.
Increasing the bias decreases the variance and vice versa. This is known
as the bias-variance dilemma.
It is difficult to simultaneously minimize both bias and variance.
As the complexity of a model increases, its bias decreases, but its variance increases.
Conversely, as the complexity of a model decreases, its bias increases, but its
variance decreases.
The optimal level of complexity for a model depends on the specific
problem and the available data.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Bias-Variance Dilemma (Trade-off)
slides.content:
Test error = Bias Squared + Variance + Irreducible Noise.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Nonlinear Classifiers:
slides.content:
✓ Analysis of the XOR problem.
✓ The Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP).

✓ The Radial Basis Function Networks (RBF).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recap: Linear Classifier – The Perceptron
slides.content:
The goal of the perceptron algorithm: obtain a linear classifier
that is applicable to new (and unknown) data:
I.e., classify the new data into the two classes (binary classification).
𝑇
IF 𝑤 𝑥 + 𝑤 > 0, assign 𝑥 to 𝑦 ;
0 1
𝑇
ELSE IF 𝑤 𝑥 + 𝑤 < 0, assign 𝑥 to 𝑦 ;
0 2
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recap: Linear Classifier – Minimize Errors
slides.content:
Our goal: design a linear classifier so that the desired outputs
of the data are ±1 – but with errors.
The true output will not always be equal to the desired output.
𝑇
Given input 𝑥, the actual output is 𝒘 𝑥, the desired output is 𝑦(𝑥).
𝒘 is computed to minimize the error between the acutual output and
the desired output.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Limitations of Linear Classifiers?
slides.content:
Focus from the performance perspective:
Linear separable – ultimately 0 errors, what about linearly inseparable?
The inseparable data should always be the minority.
Are there cases where even when we design a linear classifier, in an optimal way,
would result in poor performance – majority erroneous classification?
One example: The XOR problem.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The XOR Problem
slides.content:
Boolean functions can be interpreted as classification
tasks: depending on the input binary data, the output
is either 0 or 1 – binary classification task.
The truth table and visualization of the XOR problem
(Boolean function) shown on the right:
It is apparent that NO single straight line exists that
separates the two classes.
Compare with other Boolean functions: AND and OR;
Both are linearly separable.
The question now: how to tackle the XOR problem
and extend further to other nonlinearly separable
classification cases?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Two-Layer Perceptron
slides.content:
How to separate the two classes in the XOR problem? Any intuitive idea?
Think of leveraging a variant of the perceptron?
A first thought can be: drawing 2 lines instead of one line!
Attack the problem in two successive phases:
Calculate the position of a feature vector 𝑥
with respect to each of the two decision lines.
Combine the results of the previous phase
and we find the position of 𝑥 with respect to
both lines, i.e., outside or inside the shaded
area.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

How to realize such a successive phased solution?
First, the two decision lines, or hyperplanes, 𝑔 and 𝑔 , achieved with
1 2
the adoption of 2 perceptrons:
Outputs 𝑦 = 𝑓(𝑔 (𝑥)) where 𝑓 is the activation
𝑖 𝑖
function, a common one being the step function.
From another point of view:
Computations during the 1st phase perform a
mapping of the input vector 𝑥 to a new one 𝑦
(usually what we call a feature vector).
The decision during the second phase is now
based on the transformed data.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

IMPORTANT: the mapping of the 1st phase transforms the nonlinearly
separable problem to a linearly separable one.
The realization: through a multilayer structure – a generalization of the
perceptron: a two-layer perceptron / two-layer feedforward neural network.
𝑤
11
𝑤 𝑣
21 11
𝑤
12
𝑣
12
𝑤
22
Hidden Layer

slides.title: Capabilities of the Two-Layer Perceptron
slides.content:
The action of neurons of the hidden layer is a
mapping of the input space 𝑥 onto the vertices of
a square of unit side length in the 2d space.
𝑙
Generally: consider input vectors 𝑥 ∈ ℝ , and a
two-layer perceptron with 𝑝 neurons in the hidden
layer.
The mapping of the input space performed by the hidden
layer is now onto the vertices of the hypercube of unit
side length in the 𝑝-dim space (𝐻 ), as
𝑝
𝑇
𝑝
𝐻 = { 𝑦 , … , 𝑦 ∈ ℝ , 𝑦 ∈ 0,1 , 𝑖 ∈ [1, 𝑝]}
𝑝 1 𝑝 𝑖
The mapping of the input space onto the vertices of the
hypercube is via the creation of 𝑝 hyperplanes. Each of
which is created by a neuron in the hidden layer.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

An example of a 3-neuron hidden layer – i.e., 3 hyperplanes.
Each region defined by the intersections of the hyperplanes corresponds to a vertex
of the unit 3-dim hypercube, depending on its position w.r.t. each of the hyperplanes.
Hidden layer neurons divides the input 𝑙-dim space into polyhedra, formed by
hyperplane intersections.
All vectors located within one of these polyhedral regions are mapped onto a specific
vertex of the unit 𝐻 hypercube.
𝑝
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

An example of a 3-neuron hidden layer – i.e., 3 hyperplanes.
Output neuron realizes another hyperplane, separates the hypercube into 2 parts.
This neuron provides the 2-layer perceptron with the potential to classify vectors into
A 2-layer perceptron (a single hidden layer) can separate classes consisting of unions
classes consisting of unions of the polyhedral regions.
of polyhedral regions, but NOT any union of such regions – depends on the relative
Any failure cases?
positions of the vertices of 𝐻 , where the classes are mapped, and on whether they
𝑝
Consider class 𝐴 consists of union 000 ∪ 111 ∪ 110 and class 𝐵 the rest, can we
are linearly separable.
construct a single plane that separates class 𝐴 from class 𝐵?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Further: Multi (Three)-Layer Perceptron (MLP)
slides.content:
The inability of the 2-layer perceptrons to separate classes from any union
of polyhedral regions is similar to the problem where the basic perceptron
cannot deal with the XOR problem.
Can we opt for a similar solution?
Increase the complexity – constructing a three-layer perceptron.
Leave to think: why would the increase of one layer of neurons enable the
multi-layer perceptron to separate classes resulting from any union of
polyhedral regions?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Adjusting w.r.t. Cost Function
slides.content:
The core idea of the perceptron algorithm: adjust the synaptic
params (weights) to minimize a cost function of its output.
𝜕𝐽 𝒘

The cost function (to choose, discuss later);
The differentiation of the cost function w.r.t. the weights.
Problem?
Currently, our MLPs are developed around the McCulloch-Pitts
neurons – the hard limiter step function as activation function!
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Continuous Activation Function
slides.content:
The problem: not differentiable at some input (𝑥 = 0)!
Possible solution: finding a continuous differentiable activation
function that behaves similar to the step function.
The family of sigmoid functions;
Representative: the logistic function:
1

1 + exp −𝑎𝑥
These funcs are also known as squashing functions.

slides.content:
In actual implementation, it is actually
quite common to adopt activation
functions that are not differentiable at
some input.
In such cases, it is necessary to self-
define the gradient in the coding
(example on the right).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Training the MLP
slides.content:
Assume a multi-layer neural architecture with the sigmoid activation. The
goal: an iterative training algorithm that computes the synaptic weights of
the network so that the cost function (chosen) is minimized).
For generalization, assume 𝐿 layers of neuron, with 𝑘 = 𝑙 nodes in the
0
input layer and 𝑘 neurons in the 𝑟-th layer. Assume a total of 𝑁 training
𝑟
pairs [𝑦 𝑖 , 𝑥(𝑖)]. The output is a 𝑘 -dim output vector. The output from the
𝐿

The synaptic weights are computed such
that an appropriate cost function 𝐽, which is
dependent on the values 𝑦(𝑖) and 𝑦ො 𝑖 , 𝑖 =
1, … , 𝑁, is minimized in an iterative manner.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

1. Initialization: initialize all the weights with small random values from
a pseudo-random sequence generator.
2. Forward computations: for each of the training feature vector,

slides.title: Beyond the Basic MLP
slides.content:
The MLP we discussed (viewed as a basic MLP network) with
sigmoidal activation function produces a global hyperplane.
Discuss: what is the pros and cons of such hyperplane?
Is such hyperplane universally applicable?
In the nervous system of biological organisms, there is
evidence that responses of some neurons are "local" or tuned
to some region of input space.
An example is the orientation sensitive cells of the visual cortex, whose
response is sensitive to local region in the retina.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Radial Basis Function (RBF) Networks
slides.content:
To realize such locality behavior, introduce the Radial Basis
Function (RBF) network.
Observe: a similar structure compared to MLP, difference?
The function of the hidden layer:
MLP: sigmoidal activation function – hyperplane
RBF: radial basis function – hyper-ellipse
For each of the RBF unit (hidden layer neuron),
the operation is formulated as:
𝑑 =∥ 𝑥 − 𝑐 ∥
𝑖 𝑖
𝑜 = 𝑔 𝑑 = 𝑔 ∥ 𝑥 − 𝑐 ∥
𝑖 𝑖 𝑖
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Understanding the RBF Network
slides.content:
RBF networks emulate behavior of certain biological networks:
The hidden layer consists of the locally tuned or locally
sensitive neurons, whose response (output) is localized and
decays as a function of the distance from the input to the
center of neuron's receptive field.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: RBF Networks: The RBFs
slides.content:
One of the core of the RBF network is the choice of the RBF
(i.e., function 𝑔(⋅) of the hidden layer neuron).
Common choice of RBF include:
Gaussian function:

𝑔 𝑑 = 1/ 𝑑 2 + 𝜎2

slides.content:
The output layer neuron is a linear combiner, i.e., weighted
sum of the hidden layer neuron outputs. Assume the Gaussian
function is used for the hidden layer neurons, the output is:

What are the parameters that determine the RBF network?
The center vectors 𝑐 ;
𝑗
The weights 𝑤 corresponding to each RBF hidden neuron;
𝑗
The width of the basis function 𝜎.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Training an RBF Network: a Hybrid Process
slides.content:
There are two steps for determining the RBF:
1. Determine the RBF centers.
2. Estimation of the weights.
Usually, the overall training process is a hybrid process:
The RBF centers are determined in an unsupervised manner;
The weight estimation is computed in a supervised manner.
For determine the RBF centers, possible methods are:
Random selection;
Sequential forward selection (merge from individual neurons);
Clustering (e.g., 𝑘-means, to be discussed in Second half of course!)
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

For the weight estimation, recall LS estimation (least squares) in linear
network – the link between the hidden and output layer neurons are linear:

radial basis function (after the hidden layer neuron).
